text:
History of Science · Ongoing Research
  Pioneer Women in Climate




    For years I have been documenting the women who built the foundations of meteorology and climate science, and whose contributions were overlooked, or delayed, or attributed to others. This is a ongoing research project, and it is still growing.
    The profiles below are a just selection.
  Text extracted and adapted from the Preface of my PhD dissertation, submitted and defended in 2020, and from a peer-reviewed article published in Spanish in Tiempo y Clima (2025).





  
  
    FILE Nº 001
    
    
      
        E.N.F
      
      
        EUNICE NEWTON FOOTE
        1819 - 1888 · United States
        Physicist · Pioneered greenhouse effect research
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      In 1856, Foote demonstrated that CO₂ traps heat more than any other gas, the first experimental evidence of the greenhouse effect. Because she was a woman, she was not allowed to present her own work at the AAAS meeting. A man read it on her behalf.
      Three years later, John Tyndall published similar results and received full credit. Newton Foote's work went unrecognised for over 150 years. Today, average CO₂ levels are above 420 ppm. She predicted this would warm the Earth. She was right.
      She also participated in the Seneca Falls Convention, fighting for women's rights. Her paper "Circumstances Affecting the Heat of the Sun's Rays" was never formally published.
    
  

  
  
    FILE Nº 002
    
      
        F.M.B
      
      
        FELISA MARTÍN BRAVO
        1898 - 1979 · Spain
        Meteorologist · First woman at AEMET
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      First woman to earn a PhD in physics in Spain. In 1931 she passed the national exams to enter AEMET, becoming the first Spanish female meteorologist, and the only one until 1935.
      She collaborated with Nobel Prize winner Charles Thomson Rees Wilson at Cambridge. The Spanish Civil War halted her career, as it did for so many women scientists. Unlike most, she was allowed to return to AEMET under the dictatorship, one of only six women employed there until the 1960s.
      Between 1937 and 1940 she directed the Igeldo Meteorological Observatory, researching the violent Galernas storms of the northern Iberian coast. Between 1973 and 1974 she served as president of the Spanish Association of Meteorology, another first.
    
  

  
  
    FILE Nº 003
    
      
        J.E.L
      
      
        JEAN ELIZABETH LABY
        1915 - 2008 · Australia
        Physicist · Atmospheric aerosols & stratosphere
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      Grew up surrounded by science, her father was a professor at the University of Melbourne. WWII redirected her path: instead of joining the Bureau of Meteorology as planned, she was assigned to produce optical instruments for the armed forces.
      She worked at the University of Melbourne for over forty years. In 1959, she became the first woman to obtain a PhD in Physics in Australia. Her research covered cosmic rays, atmospheric aerosols, ozone and water vapour in the stratosphere.
      In 2009, one year after her death, she was inducted into the Victorian Honour Roll of Women.
    
  

  
  
    FILE Nº 004
    
      
        A.M
      
      
        ANNA MANI
        1918 - 2001 · India
        Meteorologist · Ozone & weather instruments
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      A voracious reader from childhood, Mani graduated with highest honours in Physics and Chemistry. Despite years of research on spectroscopy and a completed dissertation, the Madras University denied her a PhD because she lacked a master's degree.
      Undeterred, she obtained a scholarship to Imperial College London, returned to India and joined the Indian Meteorological Department. She made India self-sufficient in weather instruments, before her work, all instruments were imported from England.
      She pioneered ozone research in India long before the scientific community understood its importance, developing the first Indian ozonesonde. Her story was largely unknown outside India for decades.
    
  

  
  
    FILE Nº 005
    
      
        E.E.F
      
      
        EDITH ELIZABETH FARKAS
        1921 - 2000 · Hungary / New Zealand
        Meteorologist · Ozone monitoring · Antarctica
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      Fled Hungary during WWII, eventually emigrating to New Zealand as a refugee in 1949. Her university qualifications were not recognised, forcing her to start over, in a language she had never formally studied.
      Despite everything, she earned a Master's degree and in 1951 joined MetService, where she worked for over 35 years. She conducted world-leading ozone monitoring research for three decades using the Dobson ozone spectrophotometer.
      In 1975, she became the first woman from MetService, and one of the first women ever, to set foot in Antarctica. Upon retirement she received the MetService Henry Hill Award.
    
  

  
  
    FILE Nº 006
    
      
        J.S
      
      
        JOANNE SIMPSON
        1923 - 2010 · United States
        Meteorologist · First woman PhD in meteorology (US)
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      "Clouds are a good subject for a little girl to study." - Carl Gustav Rossby, predicting Simpson would never find a job as a meteorologist if she got her PhD.
      In 1949 she proved him wrong, becoming the first woman in the United States to hold a PhD in Meteorology. She was never hired as a meteorologist anyway,and dedicated her life to academia instead. She developed the first cloud model and co-proposed the Hot Towers hypothesis of tropical convection.
      She authored over 190 peer-reviewed articles. She worked at NOAA and NASA for decades, eventually becoming Chief Scientist for Meteorology at NASA. In 2002 she received the International Meteorological Organization Prize, the first woman ever to do so.
    
  

  
  
    FILE Nº 007
    
      
        J.B.B
      
      
        JUNE BACON-BERCEY
        1928 - 2019 · United States
        Meteorologist · First African-American woman in TV weather
        ▼ open file
      
    
    
      First African-American woman to earn a degree in atmospheric sciences in the US (UCLA, 1954) and one of the first to deliver weather on television. She feared appearing on air would cost her credibility as a scientist - "I didn't want to be seen as just a pretty face."
      She became chief meteorologist and in 1972 received the AMS Seal of Approval, the first woman and first African-American to do so. She later spent a decade as chief administrator for Television Weather Activities at NOAA.
      In 1977 she won a TV game show. She used part of her winnings to create an AGU scholarship for women studying atmospheric sciences, a legacy that outlasted her career.

← Back to maps

  Weather Station Coverage across Spain

  
    
  

    
      
        ↓ download map
      
    

  

    Choropleth map showing the distance from each Spanish municipality to the nearest Spanish Met Service (AEMET) weather station. Areas in deep blue represent distances higher than 15 km from the closest station, revealing significant coverage gaps across the territory.

    The information behind this map is the gaps in Spain’s weather-observation network, particularly in rural and mountainous areas, where investment in climate infrastructure has historically been low. Those gaps are not merely cartographic, they have real consequences.

    
      The lack of weather station coverage limits the ability to detect and anticipate extreme events such as heatwaves, heavy rainfall, and storms. Without observations, there is no data assimilation. Without data assimilation, forecast models are blind in precisely the areas that matter most during high-impact events.
    

    In the context of climate change, this is not a minor technical detail. Extreme precipitation events, prolonged droughts, and heat episodes are becoming more frequent and more intense across the Iberian Peninsula. Some of the ability to monitor those events depends on whether there is a ground station.

    
      
        
        Original LinkedIn post
      
    

    



Tools
Python · GeoPandas · Matplotlib


Context
#30DayMapChallenge 2025 · Day 7: Accessibility


Data
AEMET OpenData · Administrative boundaries CNIG-IGN





  

  
    How it was made · selected code

    1 · Fetch station data from AEMET OpenData API
    url = "https://opendata.aemet.es/opendata/api/observacion/convencional/todas"
response = requests.get(url, headers={"api_key": API_KEY})
data_url = response.json()["datos"]

observaciones = requests.get(data_url).json()
df = pd.DataFrame(observaciones)

    2 · Compute distance from each municipality to nearest station
    # Reproject to metric CRS for accurate distance calculation
municipios_m = municipios.to_crs(3035)
stations_m   = gdf_stations.to_crs(3035)

# Spatial join: nearest station per municipality
municipios_dist = municipios_m.sjoin_nearest(
    stations_m[["geometry"]],
    how="left",
    distance_col="nearest_dist_m")
municipios_dist["nearest_dist_km"] = municipios_dist["nearest_dist_m"] / 1000.0

    3 · Plot choropleth map
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 10))

municipios_dist.plot(
    ax=ax,
    column="nearest_dist_km",
    cmap=cmap,
    norm=norm,
    linewidth=0.1,
    edgecolor="white")

gdf_stations.plot(ax=ax, color="orange", marker="^",
                  markersize=8, alpha=0.6, label="AEMET stations")

ax.set_title("Areas with Fewer Nearby Spanish Met Service Weather Stations",
    fontsize=11, color="dimgray", pad=10)
ax.set_axis_off()

← Back to maps

  CO₂ and Sea Ice Extent

  
    
      
    
  

  
    
      ↓ download animation
    
  

  

    Each frame in this animation is a pair of 3D bubbles growing aornd shrinking through time to make two opposing trends. The red bubble tracks atmospheric CO₂ concentration measured at Mauna Loa: it grows relentlessly, decade after decade. The blue bubble tracks Arctic sea ice extent from the Copernicus Marine Service: it shrinks over time.

    The choice of bubbles was deliberate. A line chart can show the same data, but there is something about volume, about watching one thing expand while another collapses, that communicates the imbalance more viscerally. Climate change is not a subtle trend. The visual language should reflect that.

    
      CO₂ has risen from around 315 ppm in the late 1950s to over 420 ppm today. Over the same period, Arctic sea ice has lost roughly 13% of its extent per decade. These are not independent signals, they are two sides of the same story.
    

    
      
        
        Original LinkedIn post
      
    

    



Tools
Python · Matplotlib · FuncAnimation


Context
Climate communication · Personal project


Data
NOAA (Mauna Loa CO₂) · Copernicus Marine Service (sea ice extent)





  


  
    How it was made · PyVista code

    1 · Load data: CO2 from NOAA, sea ice from Copernicus
    url_co2 = "https://gml.noaa.gov/webdata/ccgg/trends/co2/co2_annmean_mlo.csv"
df_co2 = pd.read_csv(url_co2, comment="#", header=None,
                     names=["year", "CO2_ppm", "unc"])
df_co2["year"] = df_co2["year"].astype(int)

seaice = pd.read_csv("ARCTIC_OMI_SI_extent_obs_annual.csv")
seaice = seaice.rename(columns={"extent_million_km2": "SeaIce_million_km2"})

# Merge by year
data = pd.merge(df_co2, seaice, on="year", how="inner")
data = data[(data["year"] >= 1979) & (data["year"] <= 2023)]

    2 · Scale bubble sizes and set up the PyVista scene
    import pyvista as pv

# Normalise to [0.3, 0.9] range for bubble radius
scale_co2 = (data["CO2_ppm"] - data["CO2_ppm"].min()) / (data["CO2_ppm"].max() - data["CO2_ppm"].min()) * 0.6 + 0.3
scale_ice = (data["sea_ice_mkm2"] - data["sea_ice_mkm2"].min()) / (data["sea_ice_mkm2"].max() - data["sea_ice_mkm2"].min()) * 0.6 + 0.3

x = np.linspace(-5, 5, len(data))
y_top    = np.full_like(x, -2.0)   # CO2 row
y_bottom = np.full_like(x,  2.0)   # sea ice row

plotter = pv.Plotter(window_size=[1100, 700])
plotter.set_background("white")

    3 · Add 3D spheres and render
    reds  = plt.cm.Reds(np.linspace(0.4, 1.0, len(data)))[:, :3]
blues = plt.cm.Blues_r(np.linspace(0.3, 1.0, len(data)))[:, :3]

# CO2 bubbles: grow over time
for i in range(len(data)):
    sphere = pv.Sphere(radius=scale_co2[i], center=(x[i], y_top[i], 0))
    plotter.add_mesh(sphere, color=tuple(reds[i]),
                     smooth_shading=True, opacity=0.35,
                     specular=0.6, ambient=0.25)

# Sea ice bubbles: diminish over time
for i in range(len(data)):
    sphere = pv.Sphere(radius=scale_ice[i], center=(x[i], y_bottom[i], 0))
    plotter.add_mesh(sphere, color=tuple(blues[i]),
                     smooth_shading=True, opacity=0.4,
                     specular=0.6, ambient=0.25)

plotter.camera_position = [(8, 8, 5), (0, 0, 0), (0, 0, 1)]
plotter.show(screenshot="co2_seaice_3d_pyvista.png")

Where climate science meets Adobe Illustrator visual storytelling.




  
    
    
      an inconvenient data
      An Inconvenient Truth was one of the first climate films I watched, and one of the most striking. Released in 2006, it turned Al Gore's slideshow into a cultural moment, bringing the science of global warming to a mainstream audience long before climate communication became a field of its own.
      The poster stayed with me. So I made my own version: the factory chimneys from the original poster replaced by racks of an HPC cluster. The smoke rising from them is no longer industrial pollution, but the Ed Hawkins' Climate Stripes: blue fading into red, decade by decade. Same image, different source. Same story.
    
  

  
    
    
      fil roll X climate data
      I was born in the nineties, hence I am obsessed with the technology of that decade. Back then, photos were stored on film rolls, and before developing them, each image lived as a shadow: uncertain and dark, waiting to be revealed.
      I decided to combine that idea with current climate data. Each frame is a climate graphic from 2022, global temperature evolution and CO₂ global rise. Printed as if it were a film negative waiting to be developed. The data is there. We just had to take a look.
    
  

  
    
    
      good quality air? as retro as cassette tape
      (Again) Eighties and nineties tech combined with current air quality trends. A reminder that clean air is becoming as rare as physical media.
    
  

  
    
    
      there is no planet b
      Your phone notifies it's time to act.
    
  



  
  
    año 2021 en la tierra
    One Day on Earth is a documentary project that captures a single day on the planet through thousands of simultaneous recordings. I took that idea and made it my own: a film reel, but instead of one day, the entire 2021, told through images of climate events.
    The title is in Spanish Año 2021 en la Tierra meaning Year 2021 on Earth. What's in the frames does: a polar bear stranded on shrinking ice, wildfires tearing through landscapes across three continents, smoke where there used to be sky. Each frame a document. Each year that year.
  







  ✕

  Spanish River Basin Districts

  
    
  

  
    
      ↓ download map
    
  

  
    Map of Spain's 16 river basin districts overlaid with the HydroRIVERS dataset. Each basin is coloured independently, with river networks drawn at varying widths according to stream order. Reservoir and gauge station locations are marked as dots.

    
      
        
        Original LinkedIn post

            Self-Portrait
            Created in Adobe Illustrator using an iPad.
            ← flip back
          
        
      

      
        Hi, I'm Sabela Sanfiz.
        I am a Climate Scientist with over ten years of experience working with climate data, building visualisation products and communicating science in a meaningful way.
        My career has taken me from pure research to interactive dashboards and communication materials. That intersection — between pure science and climate storytelling — is where I am happy to be.
        Where does my work sit? Somewhere between climate science and narrative. A challenging space, but a necessary one.
